content.text: Vite (French for "fast") is a frontend build tool that serves source files over native ES modules during development and uses Rollup to bundle for production. It replaced Create React App as the community-default scaffolding tool because it starts instantly, updates changed modules in milliseconds, and produces smaller production bundles.

attrs.alt: Vite architecture: dev server uses native ESM + esbuild for fast transforms and HMR; production build uses Rollup for tree-shaking, code splitting, and hashed output
attrs.title: Vite dev server vs production build

content.text: Why Vite Instead of Create React App
Creating a New React + TypeScript Project

npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
cd my-app
npm install
npm run dev
React + JS with SWC compiler (even faster transforms)
unless you have a specific reason to avoid TypeScript. Type errors caught at build time save hours of runtime debugging.

my-app/
├── public/          # static files served as-is (favicon, robots.txt)
├── src/
│   ├── assets/      # images, fonts imported by components
│   ├── App.tsx      # root component
│   ├── App.css
│   ├── main.tsx     # entry point — mounts <App /> into #root
│   ├── index.css    # global styles
│   └── vite-env.d.ts  # Vite's import.meta.env types
├── index.html       # HTML shell — Vite injects <script type="module"> here
├── vite.config.ts
├── tsconfig.json
├── tsconfig.node.json
└── package.json
index.html is the entry point, not public/
) as the entry point for the build. It contains:
attribute is what enables native ESM in the browser during development — Vite serves
transformed to plain JS, and the browser follows import statements to load other modules on demand.
Native ESM — no bundling during development
Traditional bundlers (webpack, CRA) bundle all modules into one file before the browser can display anything. Vite skips that step: the browser requests

, Vite transforms it with esbuild (TypeScript → JavaScript), and returns the result. The browser then requests each imported module separately. Because esbuild is written in Go and operates at native speed, each transform takes microseconds.
are pre-bundled once with esbuild into
CommonJS packages (most of npm) are converted to ESM so the browser can load them.
Packages with many internal modules (e.g. lodash's 600+ files) are bundled into one file, reducing browser request count from hundreds to one.
The pre-bundle cache is invalidated automatically when
changes. Force a fresh pre-bundle with

When you save a file, Vite detects the change, transforms only that module, and sends it to the browser over a WebSocket. The browser swaps the module without a full page reload — component state is preserved. The
plugin (included in the scaffold) adds React-specific HMR via React Fast Refresh.
The default config from the scaffold is minimal:
so the TypeScript compiler resolves the alias too — Vite and tsc are independent.
Vite exposes environment variables to client-side code through
are included in the bundle (to prevent leaking server-side secrets).

# .env
VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com
VITE_APP_VERSION=1.0.0

# .env.local        — overrides .env, not committed to git
# .env.production   — used only in `npm run build`
# .env.development  — used only in `npm run dev`

npm run build      # outputs to dist/
npm run preview    # serve dist/ locally to verify before deploy

Type-check with tsc, then bundle with Rollup to dist/
Serve the production dist/ locally to test before deployment

After upgrading a dependency, if imports resolve incorrectly, run
Vite has first-class support for several CSS approaches, all without extra configuration:
is treated as a CSS Module. Class names are locally scoped — no collisions across components:
at the project root and Vite picks it up automatically. This is how Tailwind CSS integrates with Vite:

npm install --save-dev tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init -p     # creates tailwind.config.js + postcss.config.js
Rollup (the production bundler) splits the output into multiple chunks automatically when you use dynamic
. React.lazy makes this straightforward for route-level splitting:
Vite/Rollup emits a separate chunk file for each dynamic import. The browser only downloads the
chunk when the user navigates to
Vite's plugin API is compatible with Rollup plugins. Common plugins for React projects:
React Fast Refresh HMR + JSX transform (included in scaffold)
Same as above but uses SWC instead of Babel — faster on large codebases
Import SVG files as React components
Generate PWA manifest and service worker
Sync tsconfig.json paths to Vite aliases automatically
Vitest is a unit test runner built on top of Vite. It shares the same
— no separate test bundler config. It is a drop-in replacement for Jest in Vite projects.

npx vitest              # watch mode
npx vitest run          # single run (CI)
npx vitest --coverage   # coverage report
folder to any static host. Two common workflows:

npm install --save-dev vercel
npx vercel   # follow prompts — framework auto-detected as Vite

npm run build
npx gh-pages -d dist   # pushes dist/ to the gh-pages branch
Netlify and Cloudflare Pages both have zero-config Vite support — point them at the repository, set build command to
The scaffold generates two tsconfig files. Understanding what each controls prevents confusion:
Source files in src/ — strict mode, DOM types, React JSX
was introduced in TypeScript 5.0 specifically for bundler environments. It allows bare specifier imports without file extensions and supports package.json
fields — matching how Vite actually resolves modules. Older Vite templates used
is now the correct choice for Vite projects.
Vite exposes Rollup's build options through
into its own chunk means users only re-download your app code on each deployment — the React chunk stays cached as long as the React version doesn't change.

Vite's dev server serves source files as native ES modules and transforms only what the browser requests, making cold starts near-instant. In production it uses Rollup to produce content-hashed, tree-shaken bundles. The scaffold (
) gives you a working React + TypeScript project in under a minute, with
as the single customisation point for aliases, proxies, plugins, and build options.
